claims.claim: Before the battery temperature approaches the uncontrollable temperature, the gas release of LFP, LMO, and NCM batteries during thermal runaway is 0.094 mol, 0.042 mol, and 0.058 mol, respectively.
claims.quote: the gas release of LFP, LMO and NCM batteries are 0.094 mol, 0.042 mol and 0.058 mol, respectively

claims.claim: The gas released before safety venting accounts for 29.1%, 75.2%, and 55.4% of the total gas that causes the safety valve to open in LFP, LMO, and NCM batteries, respectively.
claims.quote: These gases account for 29.1, 75.2 and 55.4% of the gas volume that cause the safety valve to open in the LFP, LMO, NCM batteries, respectively

claims.claim: Safety venting of the LFP battery is mainly due to electrolyte volatilization, whereas safety venting of LMO and NCM batteries is mainly caused by gas release.
claims.quote: the safety venting of the LFP battery is mainly due to the volatilization of the electrolyte. However, the safety venting of the LMO and NCM batteries is mainly caused by the gas release

claims.claim: The NCM battery has the greatest total pressure change during thermal runaway at 9.817 MPa, which is 4.3 times the pressure change of the LFP battery.
claims.quote: The NCM battery has the greatest pressure change, P = 9.817 MPa, which is 4.3 times the pressure change of the LFP battery

claims.claim: For the LFP battery, gas release is the main cause of structural change after thermal runaway, whereas for LMO and NCM batteries, impact force is the dominant cause of pressure increase.
claims.quote: For the LFP battery, the gas release is found to be the main cause of the structural change, and for the LMO and NCM batteries, the impact force is the dominant cause

claims.claim: At 50% SoC, the LFP battery has the highest peak explosion pressure among LCO, NCA, and LFP battery types with the same SoC, followed by NCA, and LCO has the least peak pressure.
claims.quote: For a 50% SoC, the LFP battery has the maximum pressure among the three battery types with the same SoC, which is followed by the NCA, and, finally, the LCO has the least peak pressure among all of them.

claims.claim: At 50% SoC and a vent area of 0.36 m², the pressure rise from explosive ignition of LFP battery gases exceeds that of NMC battery at 100% SoC, indicating LFP batteries are more hazardous under this condition.
claims.quote: Fig. 4a also demonstrates that, at 50% SoC and a vent area of 0.36 m2, the pressure-rise due to explosive ignition of the gases ejected from the LFP battery exceeds that in the case of the NMC battery at 100% SOC, which indicates that the LFP batteries are more hazardous at this condition for the cases investigated.

claims.claim: For any equivalence ratio, increasing the enclosure vent area causes a monotonic reduction in peak explosion pressure.
claims.quote: For any ϕ, an increase in the enclosure vent area caused a monotonic reduction in the peak pressure.

claims.claim: For syngas, the laminar flame speed is highest at a rich condition rather than at stoichiometry, causing peak explosion pressure to be highest at fuel-rich conditions for LIB vented gas mixtures.
claims.quote: For syngas, the laminar flame speed is highest not at the stoichiometry but at a rich condition as depicted in Fig. 2d... the maximum pressure is observed at the fuel-rich condition, which is due to the syngas laminar flame speed behavior.

claims.claim: When CO2 is accounted for as part of the vented gas from LFP battery at 50% SoC, the equivalence ratio decreases by 32.4%, the largest reduction among the four battery cases considered, and the LFP switches from having the highest peak pressure to the lowest.
claims.quote: the largest change observed for 50% SoC LFP LIB, with 32.4% reduction in its equivalence ratio... The peak pressure switched from the LFP having the highest peak pressure to the lowest peak pressure as shown in Fig. 8.

claims.claim: When flaming combustion occurs during battery thermal runaway, the effective heat of combustion jumps to 50 kJ/Wh, compared to 6–12 kJ/Wh when no flaming combustion is taking place.
claims.quote: When no flaming combustion is taking place, the effective heat of combustion is comprised between 6 and 12 kJ/Wh, reflecting very incomplete combustion processes driven by semi-oxidative environment created in a first step with oxygen released by the cathodic transition metal oxide mix. In contrast, when flames are visible, the effective heat of combustion jumps to 50 kJ/Wh.

claims.claim: During the fume phase (no flames), the CO/CO2 ratio is close to 0.8, illustrating a dominant pyrolytic process, whereas in the presence of flames the CO/CO2 ratio is close to 0.01, indicating good combustion not limited by O2 availability.
claims.quote: The ratio CO/CO2 (vol.) is close to 0.01 in the presence of flame showing a good combustion, not limited by the O2 availability. On the contrary, in the first phase, when only fumes are visible, the CO/CO2 ratio is close to 0.8, illustrating a dominant pyrolytic process.

claims.claim: When flames appear during battery thermal runaway, CO2 production increases by a factor of 8, HF production increases by a factor of 10, and NOx production increases by a factor of 4, while CH4 decreases by a factor of 12, C2H4 decreases by a factor of 7, CO decreases by a factor of 10, POF3 decreases by a factor of 4, carbonates decrease by a factor of 7, and CH2O and H2 almost disappear.
claims.quote: gaseous species produced in larger quantities after the flame appearance. It is the case of CO2 (x8); HF (x10) and NOx (x4). 2- gaseous species produced in smaller quantities after the flame appearance as witnessed for CH4 (/12), C2H4 (/7), CO (/10), POF3 (/4), carbonates (/7), CH2O (almost disappearing) and H2 (almost disappearing).

claims.claim: At module level, the simultaneous reaction of two adjacent cells produced a peak heat release rate of 250 kW, compared to 100–130 kW peaks at cluster level where cells reacted sequentially, even though only 3 cells participated in the reaction in both cases.
claims.quote: At cluster level, three peaks are identifiable. A small one (20 kW), attributed to the reaction of the first cell with no flames, and two bigger ones (100–130 kW) related to the reaction of the two neighbor cells, in the presence of fire. At module level only two peaks are visible, a small one (25 kW) also associated with the reaction of the first cell with no flames, and a second large one (250 kW) due to the simultaneous reaction of the two adjacent cells.

claims.claim: The time at which individuals may experience compromised tenability is assessed to be two times shorter in the case of flaming combustion compared to cell degassing without flaming combustion, because critical HF production is bound to fire development.
claims.quote: the time at which individuals may be expected to experience compromised tenability is assessed to be two time shorter in case of fire as compared to cell degassing emission only.
